Section 182l5(b) (1). 
However, it is my understanding from 
your conversation with Dan Lowenstein and my conversation 
with your assistant Marcy Kates that the dinner will not be 
held to accumulate funds that the Assemb1 p nan will use for 
any political purposes, such as paying off past debts or 
making contributions to other candidates in elections. 
Moreover, since the Assemblyman is retiring, he will not use 
the receipts to participate in future campaigns. 
In the instant case we understand the only items 
of value the Assemblyman ~}ll receive are one free ticket 
and other possible gifts.-
Thus, in this particular situa-
tion, the Assemblyman receives a benefit which is more ap-
propriately characterized as a gift than as a contribution. 
The Act requires disclosure of any gift of $25 or 
more on the Assemblyman's Statement of Economic Interests. 
The value of the gift received by the Assemblyman is the per 
capita cost of giving the dinner. 
Opinion requested by 
Annie Gutierrez, 3 FPPC Opinions 44 (No. 76-081, June 7, 
1977). 
(A copy of that Opinion is enclosed.) 
You have 
informed us that the dinner will cost approx 
tely $13 per 
person but that certain additional costs will be incurred, 
such as the expenses of printing invitations and purchasing 
decorations. 
Thus, the per 
ita cost should be deterrnin 
by calculating the total cost of the event, including the 
additional expenses, and dividing that amount by the number 
of persons who attend. 
If the per capita cost of the event 
is less than $25, the retiring Assemblyman has no reporting 
obligation. 
However, if any part of the proceeds is used to 
purchase a gift for the Assemblyman 
he should combine the 
per capita cost of the event with the value of the gift. 
The gifts are reportable if the aggregate value equals or 
exceeds $25. 
The legislator is not required to disclose the 
name of each donor so long as no individual donor's share of 
the gifts is $25 or more. 
Instead, he may include an appro-
priate designation that accurately describes the group of 
people making the gift. 
Opinion requested by Art Torres, 
2 FPPC Opinions 31 (No. 75-163, Feb. 4, 1976). 
2 
Although the Assemblyman will also receive an 
intangible 
nefit by 
ing honored, we do not believe that 
benefit need be 
alued and reported in this situa ion.

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

Subject: Letter to Assemblyman John T. Knox 
Attached find a draft letter to Knox. 
Please 
read it, especially the response to question 2. 
The 
specific question I have is whether the gift from the 
lobbyist is equal to the payments by lobbyists divided 
by number of people who attend, or the payments by all 
persons divided by number of persons who attend;

plus $2 for gift and extras.

sections.question: 1. What reporting obligations, if any, will the retiring Assemblyman incur as a result of the dinner? 2. What restrictions apply to lobbyists who wish to participate in planning the dinner and to pay for the cost of printing invitations or other costs associated with the dinner?
sections.conclusion: With respect to the first issue, the retiring Assemblyman receives a benefit more appropriately characterized as a gift than a contribution. If the per capita cost of the event (including all costs divided by attendees) is less than $25, there is no reporting obligation. If $25 or more, it must be disclosed on the Statement of Economic Interests; the legislator need not name individual donors if no donor's share is $25 or more. With respect to the second issue, Section 86203 prohibits lobbyists from making gifts aggregating more than $10 per calendar month or arranging gifts. Because the testimonial dinner is a common plan providing benefits exceeding $10, lobbyists cannot participate in the planning group or make payments for specific costs. However, lobbyists may purchase tickets and attend if the ticket cost includes at most $2 attributable as a gift.
sections.question_synthetic: The letter addresses reporting requirements and restrictions under the Political Reform Act for a testimonial dinner honoring a retiring Assembly member, including what the retiring member must disclose and what limitations apply to lobbyist participation and contributions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The retiring Assemblyman must report the dinner as a gift if the per capita cost equals or exceeds $25, without naming individual donors. Lobbyists are prohibited from participating in planning the dinner or paying for specific costs, but may purchase tickets if only $2 per ticket constitutes a gift.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the formal advice letter and an internal FPPC memorandum discussing the draft response. Some text is degraded/OCR-imperfect in the source document. The core Q/A sections are present and clearly identifiable. Enclosed materials and footer memorandum provide context but are supplementary to the main advice letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. What reporting obligations, if any, will the retiring Assemblyman incur as a result of the dinner? 2. What restrictions apply to lobbyists who wish to participate in planning the dinner and to pay for the cost of printing invitations or other costs associated with the dinner?

CONCLUSION: With respect to the first issue, the retiring Assemblyman receives a benefit more appropriately characterized as a gift than a contribution. If the per capita cost of the event (including all costs divided by attendees) is less than $25, there is no reporting obligation. If $25 or more, it must be disclosed on the Statement of Economic Interests; the legislator need not name individual donors if no donor's share is $25 or more. With respect to the second issue, Section 86203 prohibits lobbyists from making gifts aggregating more than $10 per calendar month or arranging gifts. Because the testimonial dinner is a common plan providing benefits exceeding $10, lobbyists cannot participate in the planning group or make payments for specific costs. However, lobbyists may purchase tickets and attend if the ticket cost includes at most $2 attributable as a gift.

embedding.summary: This advice letter responds to Assemblyman John T. Knox's inquiry regarding reporting obligations and restrictions under the Political Reform Act for a testimonial dinner honoring a retiring Assembly member. The letter clarifies the characterization of the benefit as a gift requiring disclosure above $25 and prohibits lobbyist participation in planning or funding specific dinner costs.
